main_summary.summary: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan, born June 28, 1956 in Lincolnville, St. Augustine, Florida, is a fifth-generation native and co-founder of the 40th ACCORD organization. Interviewed September 13, 2011 by Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program for the Civil Rights History Project. Duncan traces her family lineage to her great-great grandfather Thomas A. Finley, who migrated from the Carolinas and settled in Moultrie and Lincolnville. She recounts childhood memories of Ku Klux Klan marches through Lincolnville, witnessing the colored sign at the local doctor's building, and the forced integration of St. Augustine schools that closed Excelsior Elementary in her sixth-grade year. Duncan describes her work, alongside Mrs. Cora Tyson Johnson and historian David Nolan, organizing the 40th anniversary commemoration of the St. Augustine civil rights movement following Mrs. Catherine Twine's death in 2002. Through 40th ACCORD she helped fund 30 historic civil rights markers via Northrop Grumman, organized teacher workshops with Dr. Robert B. Hayling and Lillian Twine Robeson, and is working toward establishing a civil rights museum in St. Augustine by the 50th anniversary in 2014.
key_themes: Historic civil rights markers and memory work
main_summary.historical_significance:
Duncan provides a child's-eye witness account of the St. Augustine campaign, including direct recollections of Klan marches through Lincolnville, the social experience of forced integration, and the personal encounter with Dr. King at Mr. Proctor's house. Her testimony documents the lived continuity between segregation-era memory and contemporary memorial activism in St. Augustine.

As a co-organizer of 40th ACCORD, Duncan is also a primary source for the institutional history of how the St. Augustine movement was publicly commemorated decades after the 1964 events. Her account names the founding meeting at South Seas restaurant, the Northrop Grumman funding of 30 historic markers, and the teacher workshops with Dr. Robert B. Hayling, providing documentary detail about how local Black activists rebuilt the historical record against what she calls the 'hidden vapor' of suppressed civic memory.

chapters.title: Introduction and Family Genealogy
chapters.summary: Joe Mosnier introduces the interview at the Bayfront Hilton in St. Augustine on September 13, 2011. Duncan describes her birth in Lincolnville in 1956 and traces her family back five generations to her great-great grandfather Thomas A. Finley, who migrated from the Carolinas through South Carolina and Georgia to Moultrie. Her great-grandmother Abby Finley was a founder of St. Cyprian's Episcopal Church in Lincolnville.
related_events: Family migration from the Carolinas to St. Augustine
Founding of St. Cyprian's Episcopal Church

chapters.title: Growing Up in Segregated Lincolnville
chapters.summary: Duncan describes life in segregated Lincolnville as a child of eight sisters. She recounts the Black-owned businesses on Washington Street, the centrality of the church, and her great-grandmother who died in November 1963 the same month as President Kennedy's assassination.
related_events: Assassination of President Kennedy

chapters.title: Klan Marches and Dr. King's Visit
chapters.summary: At seven and a half years old, Duncan witnessed Ku Klux Klan marches through Lincolnville, which she initially mistook for a parade. She recalls Black residents lining the streets at Central Avenue (now Martin Luther King Avenue), classmates' homes being firebombed, and her mother holding her sister's hand telling her not to look back. She remembers Dr. King coming across the street from Mr. Proctor's house to ask her mother if she would participate in the marches.
related_events: St. Augustine civil rights campaign 1964
Klan night marches through Lincolnville
Firebombing of Black homes

chapters.title: The Colored Sign at the Doctor's Office
chapters.summary: Around ages 10-12, Duncan ventured downtown and experienced segregation firsthand at the doctor's building near Flagler Hospital. She describes the colored sign on the wall pointing Black patients around to the back entrance, and later, after the sign came down following the Civil Rights Act, taking her younger sister to the doctor through the previously forbidden front hallway and being met with hostile stares.
related_events: Passage of the Civil Rights Act of 1964

chapters.title: Forced School Integration
chapters.summary: Duncan completed fifth grade at Excelsior Elementary School before forced integration closed the school. She describes the white teachers' refusal to teach, classmates' refusal to sit beside her, and the violence at the junior high school where students had to walk back to Orange Street to eat because the junior high had no cafeteria, with white students forming lines to block them. She graduated from St. Augustine High School.
related_events: Forced school integration in St. Augustine

chapters.title: Founding of 40th ACCORD
chapters.summary: Following Mrs. Catherine Twine's death in 2002, David Nolan, JT Johnson from Atlanta, Jane Billings of Brown (Spelman professor), Mrs. Cora Tyson Johnson, Dwight Hines, and Sandra Parks met at South Seas restaurant to plan the 40th anniversary of the St. Augustine civil rights movement. Duncan proposed framing the commemoration as a family reunion to bring participants back to St. Augustine. She and Mrs. Cora Tyson Johnson set up a strategic meeting at the Galimore Center where older residents came out and shared their stories.
related_events: Death of Mrs. Catherine Twine (2002)
Founding meeting of 40th ACCORD
First family reunion 2001

chapters.title: Bringing the Movement's Memory Back
chapters.summary: Duncan recounts JT Johnson's response at a meeting when a demonstrator asked why bring this history up again: Johnson said it was time to just say thank you. Duncan reflects on St. Augustine's suppression of its Black history, the founding of Fort Mose as the first free Black settlement in the United States, and her grief that despite the sacrifices of Dr. Robert Hayling and Dr. King, much remains unchanged in the town.
related_events: Founding of Fort Mose as first free Black settlement

chapters.title: Northrop Grumman Funds the Civil Rights Markers
chapters.summary: Duncan describes how Northrop Grumman funded 30 historic civil rights markers in and around St. Augustine after she wrote a letter requesting funding. The previous occupant of the site, identified as Fairchild, had been hostile to civil rights demonstrators and Black employees. Markers honor Dr. Robert Hayling, the St. Augustine Foot Soldiers, Audrey Nell Hamilton, JoeAnn Anderson, and Mrs. Reena Ayers, who at 107 had given lodging and food to demonstrators and Northern attorneys.
related_events: Installation of 30 historic civil rights markers in St. Augustine

chapters.title: What Has Not Changed in St. Augustine
chapters.summary: Asked what problems remain, Duncan cites the absence of jobs and opportunities for Black residents, the lack of Black businesses (fewer than one hand could count), minimal Black representation in city government, and the departure of young people. Her son with engineering degrees from the University of Florida could not find work in St. Augustine and now lives in Jacksonville. Her daughter, a doctor of physical therapy, is considering leaving.
related_events: Departure of Black young professionals from St. Augustine

chapters.title: Freedom Trail Markers and Ambassador Young
chapters.summary: Duncan discusses the city's reaction to the Freedom Trail markers and Foot Soldiers Monument. She credits Ambassador Andrew Young's film 'Crossing in St. Augustine' with bringing national exposure that shifted local attitudes. She suggests the city's eventual embrace of the history is partly motivated by tourism revenue, but acknowledges that at minimum the city did not block the private foot soldiers monument project.
related_events: Premiere of Ambassador Young's 'Crossing in St. Augustine' film
Installation of Foot Soldiers Monument

chapters.title: St. Augustine's Future and Industry
chapters.summary: Duncan reflects on St. Augustine as a quiet town she loved raising a family in, while citing Hank Thomas's description of the city as paternalistic. She argues that while the historic city should remain old, St. Johns County needs industry so Black families can return. Thousands of Black residents have left and she wants to see the community restored.
related_events: Demographic decline of Black St. Augustine community

chapters.title: Teacher Workshops and the Museum Vision
chapters.summary: Duncan describes 40th ACCORD's upcoming Civil Rights Act anniversary commemorations including past speaker Dr. Dorothy Cotton, Sonja Plummer (granddaughter of Reverend Goldie Eubanks), and upcoming speaker Taylor Branch in July 2012. She details two teacher workshops conducted with St. Johns County, one at St. Augustine High School featuring Dr. Robert Hayling and Lillian Twine Robeson, funded by Northrop Grumman. Long-term, the organization aims to establish a civil rights museum in St. Augustine by the 50th anniversary in 2014, with Richard Burton (NAACP) and JT Johnson as co-chairs.
related_events: Teacher workshops with St. Johns County
Planning for 50th anniversary of Civil Rights Act (2014)
Taylor Branch speaking event July 2012

loc_video.caption: Gwendolyn Annette Duncan oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Saint Augustine, Florida, 2011 September 14
